indicator.formula_as_reported: BGVI = sum(viewshed-visible green pixels) per building observation point (viewshed algorithm on 1 m DEM); SVGVI = sum(green_pixels)/sum(total_pixels) across 18 BSV images per site

relationship.description: BGVI > SVGVI at ~76.10 % of sample sites in Tiexi District, Shenyang; vertical (building-eye) visible greenery exceeds street-eye visible greenery in a high-rise compact city.

descriptive_content: Paired BGVI vs SVGVI comparison across 800 street-level sample sites in Tiexi District, Shenyang (PR China). BGVI computed via viewshed algorithm on a 1 m DEM; SVGVI computed via FCN semantic segmentation of 14,400 Baidu Street View images (18 per site). Result: BGVI > SVGVI at ~76.10 % of sites.

descriptive_content: Visible vibrant green space differs significantly between top-quartile (q4) and bottom-quartile (q1) safety perception locations (q4 mean = 19.10%, SD = 13.10; q1 mean = 12.09%, SD = 12.12; t-test difference = 7.01, p < 0.001). The same pattern holds for lively and beauty quartiles. Among image-based features, vibrant green space (alongside visible green space and visible trees) was repeatedly highlighted as one of the variables that differed most strongly between high and low perception quartiles. In Lasso regression models, vibrant green space was retained as a statistically significant predictor of safety, lively, and beauty perceptions; spatial cluster of greenspace + vibrant greenspace + visible greenspace together reduced explained variation by 2.51%, 2.80%, and 0.76% for safety, lively, and beauty respectively when removed.

descriptive_content: Plant Color Richness (PCR) was operationalised as an entropy-saturation visual color richness metric applied only to plant pixels after semantic segmentation, following Han's method (Eq. for C with M = sigma_rgyb + 0.3*mu_rgyb). Compared with GVI and PLD, PCR showed a less pronounced and more ambiguous SHAP trend: high-PCR samples produced both positive and negative SHAP values for pleasure (i.e. mixed effect). For relaxation, when PCR > 15 SHAP values fell in the [-0.6, +0.2] band, suggesting an inhibitory (negative) tendency at high color richness. For boredom, PCR in 0-10 concentrated in the positive-SHAP region (low PCR promotes boredom) while PCR in 15-30 concentrated in the negative-SHAP region (high PCR suppresses boredom). For anxiety the trend was unclear.

descriptive_content: Motorization Degree (MD) is defined as the sum of pixel proportions for road, car, bus, truck, freight vehicle, small motor vehicle and railway over total image pixels (Eq. 6). It is one of six evaluation indicators (alongside SVI sky visibility, GVI green visual, IEI interface enclosure, PCI public-facility convenience, TR traffic recognition) and is treated as a NEGATIVE indicator in the composite spatial-quality score (i.e. higher MD => lower quality). Spatial mapping in ArcGIS showed that the overall motorization level of the entire study area is 'not optimistic' and that high MD coincides with low SVI and low GVI in arterial corridors; the downtown core scores high overall quality despite relatively low GVI/IEI/PCI/MD performance.

descriptive_content: Plant Level Diversity (PLD) was defined as the diversity of plant landscape strata (grass, flowers, shrubs/plants, trees) and operationalised in three forms (count N, Shannon entropy, Simpson). PLD had the highest mean SHAP value across the four greening indicators (mean SHAP = 0.225), surpassing GVI (0.208) and ranking it as the single most important predictor of emotional perception. Higher PLD values correspond to stronger positive impact on pleasure and relaxation and stronger suppression of boredom and anxiety; the relationship is non-linear with a marked transition around PLD = 0.2-0.4 and a strongest effect once PLD > 0.9. The Simpson form did not show substantial influence on emotion. The richness/entropy forms drove most of the effect.

descriptive_content: Foreground Edge Roughness Index (S_ERI = 0.25 * P / sqrt(A)) is a relative-relationship shape metric derived from the isoperimetric inequality. In framed scenery the foreground holds regular outlines (low S_ERI) with smooth depth continuity (high P_ECI), and the two are positively correlated (r = 0.656, p < 0.01). Mean S_ERI = 1.634; Mdn = 0.986; 95% CI [1.300, 1.968]. Lower S_ERI values dominate framed scenery, supporting the design rule that framing structures need geometrically stable foreground outlines.

The metric is reported descriptively as part of the relative-relationship shape family and contributes to discriminating obstructive/porous scenery (which use textural contrast) from framed/sandwiched scenery.

descriptive_content: Foreground Largest-Hole Asymmetry Index (S_ADI = (SDi - SDj)/SDi) quantifies whether one hole inside the foreground dominates the others. In framed scenery, mean S_ADI = 0.957, 95% CI [0.926, 0.988] (left-skewed near maximum 1, Sk = -3.806, K = 16.2) - i.e. there is normally one dominant 'frame opening' hole. Within framed scenery S_ADI correlates negatively with P_ECI (r = -0.66, p < 0.01), meaning when a single hole dominates the foreground (high S_ADI), depth continuity P_ECI tends to be lower (smoother depth transition through that single window). Author also reports S_ADI accounts for ~12.1% feature importance and S_VFR ~9.9% in the random-forest archetype classifier.

relationship.description: Visual Greenery Vector / Field (VGF) defined/used by paper; no inferential association recorded in this minimal record.

descriptive_content: The Visual Greenery Vector / Field (VGF) is a 2D vector at each sample point with v_x = GVI_E - GVI_W and v_y = GVI_N - GVI_S; its magnitude |v| = sqrt(v_x^2 + v_y^2) expresses the strength of directional asymmetry in greenery and theta = atan2(v_y, v_x) expresses the dominant direction of green visibility. In the Milan NIL 22 Citta Studi case, validation of the underlying directional GVI showed automatic vs manual segmentation correlation = 0.97 (p < 0.001), indicating near-perfect linear agreement; the VGF then revealed significant cardinal-direction differences in greenery (Friedman + post-hoc), and significant positive spatial autocorrelation of greenery (Moran's-style p < 0.05), confirming that the VGF captures real anisotropy that the omnidirectional GVI hides.

relationship.description: Foreground Maximum Depth Value (P_LV) defined/used by paper; no inferential association recorded in this minimal record.

descriptive_content: Foreground Maximum Depth Value (P_LV = max Di in foreground) is an absolute-value position metric on a 1-256 grayscale derived from MiDaS monocular depth maps. In framed scenery, mean P_LV = 136.185; Mdn = 148.5; 95% CI [250.213, 254.343]; 'above median of 128' indicates extended depth in the foreground. P_LV correlates positively with P_LN (r = 0.656, p < 0.01): foregrounds that start near the observer also extend deep in. In random-forest models, P_LV contributes ~24.3% feature importance for obstructive scenery, reflecting that obstructive scenery 'utilizes systematic positioning with texture contrasts' anchored at high P_LV.

relationship.description: Foreground Vertical Depth Length (P_LN) defined/used by paper; no inferential association recorded in this minimal record.

descriptive_content: Foreground Vertical Depth Length (P_LN = count of unique non-repeating depth values in the foreground) is an absolute-value position metric capturing the vertical depth extent. In framed scenery, mean P_LN = 50; Mdn = 255; 95% CI [119.441, 152.929], near the maximum 256, indicating optimal alignment with observer position. P_LN correlates with T_ESI (r = 0.594, p < 0.01) reinforcing hierarchical contrast in space, and with P_LV (r = 0.656, p < 0.01). The metric is interpreted as encoding the way the foreground 'starts close and extends to sufficient depth'.

indicator.formula_as_reported: P_ECI = sqrt( (1/N) * sum_j (CV_j - mu_CV)^2 ) / mu_CV ; CV_j = sqrt((1/Mj)*sum_i(Cij - mu_j)^2) / mu_j ; Cij = |idx_i - idx_{i+1}|

variables_as_reported.Cij: absolute difference in depth values between the i-th pair of neighboring pixels in the j-th row or column
variables_as_reported.mu_j: mean of all Cij in the j-th row or column
variables_as_reported.CV_j: coefficient of variation of all Cij in the j-th row or column
variables_as_reported.mu_CV: average of CV_j across all rows and columns
variables_as_reported.Mj: number of Cij in the j-th row or column
variables_as_reported.N: total number of rows and columns in the foreground

performance.outcome_measure: Random-forest classification of scenic archetype (esp. framed scenery, smooth depth)

effect_size.type: Pearson correlation r between low S_ERI (regular outline) and high P_ECI (smooth depth) within framed scenery
effect_size.description: Descriptive: paper provides formula but no inferential effect

relationship.description: Foreground Depth Continuity Index (P_ECI) defined/used by paper; no inferential association recorded in this minimal record.

descriptive_content: Foreground Depth Continuity Index (P_ECI) is a distributional-trend metric defined as the coefficient of variation of the per-row/column coefficients of variation of |idx_i - idx_{i+1}| absolute depth differences across the foreground. P_ECI = 0 means continuously consistent depth; larger P_ECI means more abrupt depth transitions. In framed scenery, mean P_ECI = 1.237; Mdn = 1.143; 95% CI [1.074, 1.400] (lower-range concentration => smooth depth transitions). P_ECI correlates positively with low S_ERI (r = 0.656, p < 0.01) and negatively with S_ADI (r = -0.66, p < 0.01), supporting framed scenery's signature of regular outline plus smooth depth.

relationship.description: Street trees in downtown Boston decrease the SVF by an average of 18.52% (SVF_S minus SVF_P across 297 GSV panorama sites). The reduction quantifies the shading effect attributable specifically to street-tree canopies, isolating it from the obstruction by building blocks.

performance.outcome_measure: Agreement between SVF_P (GSV photographic) and SVF_S (building-height simulation) at sites with no tree canopy within 30 m, used to validate the GSV-based method

effect_size.unit: regression of SVF_P on SVF_S, dimensionless SVF

relationship.description: Validation analysis: at sites with no surrounding tree canopy within 30 m, the GSV-based photographic SVF (SVF_P) and the building-height-model simulated SVF (SVF_S) are tightly correlated (r-square = 0.90, RMSE = 0.084), supporting the GSV-photographic method as an accurate SVF estimator. At sites with surrounding tree canopy, the two SVF estimates diverge (r-square = 0.68), with SVF_P consistently lower than SVF_S, isolating the shade contribution of trees.

descriptive_content: This record describes spatiotemporal evolution patterns based on cluster analysis, showing cluster migration in perceived dimensions of the urban visual environment. Specifically, over time, samples shift from clusters with higher perceived quality (Cluster 2, 3, 4) to a cluster with declining perceived quality (Cluster 1). This is a descriptive finding about patterns of change in GVI and the urban perceived environment, rather than a quantified correlation or causal effect analysis. Fig. 13 visualizes the spatiotemporal distribution of clusters.

relationship.description: Vibrant Green Space defined/used by paper; no inferential association recorded in this minimal record.

descriptive_content: Visible vibrant green space differs significantly between top-quartile (q4) and bottom-quartile (q1) safety perception locations (q4 mean = 19.10%, SD = 13.10; q1 mean = 12.09%, SD = 12.12; t-test difference = 7.01, p < 0.001). The same pattern holds for lively and beauty quartiles. Among image-based features, vibrant green space (alongside visible green space and visible trees) was repeatedly highlighted as one of the variables that differed most strongly between high and low perception quartiles. In Lasso regression models, vibrant green space was retained as a statistically significant predictor of safety, lively, and beauty perceptions; spatial cluster of greenspace + vibrant greenspace + visible greenspace together reduced explained variation by 2.51%, 2.80%, and 0.76% for safety, lively, and beauty respectively when removed.

relationship.description: Vibrant Green Space defined/used by paper; no inferential association recorded in this minimal record.

descriptive_content: Visible vibrant green space differs significantly between top-quartile (q4) and bottom-quartile (q1) safety perception locations (q4 mean = 19.10%, SD = 13.10; q1 mean = 12.09%, SD = 12.12; t-test difference = 7.01, p < 0.001). The same pattern holds for lively and beauty quartiles. Among image-based features, vibrant green space (alongside visible green space and visible trees) was repeatedly highlighted as one of the variables that differed most strongly between high and low perception quartiles. In Lasso regression models, vibrant green space was retained as a statistically significant predictor of safety, lively, and beauty perceptions; spatial cluster of greenspace + vibrant greenspace + visible greenspace together reduced explained variation by 2.51%, 2.80%, and 0.76% for safety, lively, and beauty respectively when removed.
